title: Vegetable Kurma

overview.description: A fragrant South Indian vegetable kurma with mixed vegetables in a creamy coconut, cashew and aromatic spice sauce.

ingredients: 400g mixed vegetables, cut into bite-sized pieces
method: Soak the cashews in hot water for 15–20 minutes.
Add the soaked cashews, desiccated coconut, green chillies, poppy seeds if using and enough water to a blender.
Blend until as smooth as possible and set aside.
Heat the oil or ghee in a large saucepan over a medium heat.
Add the cinnamon, cardamom, cloves and bay leaf.
Fry for approximately 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the sliced onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the turmeric, ground coriander and cumin.
Add the mixed vegetables and stir to coat in the spices.
Add the coconut milk and water.
Cover and cook until the vegetables are almost tender.
Simmer gently for 5–10 minutes until the sauce has thickened and the vegetables are fully tender.
Remove the whole spices before serving if desired.

learning.did-you-know: The word kurma refers to a family of dishes found across South Asia, with recipes varying significantly between regions. South Indian versions often make prominent use of coconut.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a smooth nut and coconut paste, layer whole and ground spices, and build a rich curry sauce without relying on dairy.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A mild, rich South Indian curry that introduces the children to the use of nuts and coconut to create texture and richness in a sauce.
chapman-tips: Soaking the cashews makes it much easier to blend the kurma paste smoothly.
Blend the paste as smoothly as your equipment allows; a smoother paste produces a more luxurious sauce.
Don't rush the onions. Their sweetness forms an important part of the base.
Add the kurma paste towards the end so the coconut and nuts do not catch on the bottom of the pan.
Use vegetables with different cooking times by adding harder vegetables first.
The curry should be rich and aromatic rather than aggressively hot.
notes.batch-cooking: Cool completely before freezing in portions. The sauce may thicken after freezing, so loosen with a little water when reheating.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with rice or flatbreads. Leftover kurma can also be used as a filling for wraps or stuffed flatbreads.
